<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] {{Short description|Japanese term for gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:NikkoToshoguOmotemon5230.jpg|thumb|270 px|[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]'s ''omote-mon'' (front gate) structurally is a ''hakkyakumon'' (eight-legged gate)]]
{{nihongo|'''''Mon'''''|門|gate}} is a generic Japanese term for [[:en:Gate|Gate]] often used, either alone or as a suffix, in referring to the many gates used by [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and traditional-style buildings and castles.
==Significance== Unlike gates of secular buildings, most temple and shrine gates are purely symbolic elements of [[:en:Liminality|Liminality]], as they cannot be completely closed and just mark the transition between the mundane and the sacred.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Carson |first= Timothy L. |chapter= Seven: Betwixt and Between, Worship and Liminal Reality |title= Transforming Worship |place= St. Louis, MO |publisher= Chalice |year= 2003 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Turner |first= Victor |chapter= Betwixt and Between: The Liminal Period in Rites de Passage |title= The Forest of Symbols |place= Ithaca, NY |publisher= Cornell University Press |year= 1967}}</ref> In many cases, for example that of the ''[[sanmon]]'', a temple gate has purifying, cleansing properties.
==Description== Gate size is measured in ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'', where a ''ken'' is the interval between two pillars of a traditional-style building. A temple's ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' for example can have dimensions from a maximum of 5x2 ''ken'' to a more common 3x2 ''ken'', down to even one ''ken''.<ref>{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | language=ja |page=79}}</ref> The word is usually translated in English as "bay" and is better understood as an indication of proportions than as a unit of measurement.
Like the temples they belong to, gates can be in the ''[[:en:Wayō|Wayō]]'', ''[[daibutsuyō]]'', ''[[:en:Zenshūyō|Zenshūyō]]'' or ''[[setchūyō]]'' style.<ref name="jaanusmon">{{cite web|title=Mon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=27 August 2010}}</ref> They can be named after: * Their location, as the {{nihongo|''chūmon''|中門|lit. "intermediate gate"}} or of the {{nihongo|omotemon|表門|lit. front gate}} or the {{nihongo|karametemon|搦手門|lit. back entrance gate}}. * The deity they house, as the ''[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]'' (lit. "Niō gate", see below), a gate enshrining two gods called [[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]] in its outer bays. * Their structure or shape, as the ''[[:en:Nijūmon|Nijūmon]]'' (lit. "two-story gate", see below) and the ''rōmon'' (lit. tower gate). * Their function, as the ''[[sanmon]]'' (see below), which is the most important gate of a [[:en:Zen|Zen]] or [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] temple.
Not all such terms are mutually exclusive and the same gate may be called with different names according to the situation. For example, a ''Niōmon'' can also be correctly called a ''nijūmon'' if it has two stories.
==Variations== Very different structurally from the others is the ''[[torii]]mon'' (normally called simply ''torii''), a two-legged gate in stone or wood regularly associated with Shinto, but common also within Japanese Buddhist temples.<ref name="Shinto"/> As prominent a temple as [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]'s [[Shitennō-ji]], founded in 593 by [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] and the oldest state-built Buddhist temple in the country, has a ''torii'' straddling one of its entrances.<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=12 February 2010}}</ref> The origins of the ''torii'' are unknown; although several theories on the subject exist, none has gained universal acceptance.<ref name=Shinto>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=280|title=Torii|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]]| date=2005-06-02| access-date=2010-02-21}}</ref> Because the use of symbolic gates is widespread in Asia—such structures can be found for example in [[:en:India|India]], [[:en:China|China]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]], [[:en:Thailand‏‎|Thailand‏‎]], [[:en:Korea|Korea]], and within [[Nicobarese people|Nicobarese]] and [[:en:Shompen people|Shompen]] villages—historians believe it may be an imported tradition. It most often symbolically marks the entrance of a Shinto shrine. For this reason, it is never closed.
==Common types== * {{nihongo|''Hakkyakumon'' or ''Yatsuashimon''|八脚門|eight-legged gate}} – so called because of its eight secondary pillars, which support four main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has twelve pillars altogether.<ref>{{cite web|title=Hakkyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hakkyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Heijūmon''|塀重門}} – A gate in a wall consisting in just two square posts.<ref name="castle.info"/> *{{nihongo|''Kabukimon''|冠木門}} – A gate in a wall formed by two square posts and a horizontal beam.<ref>{{cite web|title=Kabukimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kabukimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''[[Karamon]]''|唐門|chinese gate}} – A gate characterized by a ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan.<ref name="jaanuskaramon">{{cite web|title=Karamon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> ''Karamon'' are used at [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s. *{{nihongo|''Kōraimon''|高麗門|lit. Korean gate}} – Used at castles, temples and ''daimyō'' residences, it consists of a tiled, gabled roof on two pillars, plus two smaller roofs over the {{nihongo|secondary pillars|控柱|hikaebashira}} on the rear of the gate.<ref name="jaanuskaramon"/> * {{nihongo|''Masugata''|枡形}}. A defensive structure consisting in a courtyard along the wall of a castle with two gates set at a square angle, one giving access to the castle and one facing the outside. The external gate is typically a ''kōraimon'', the internal one a ''yaguramon''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Masugata|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/masugata.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> The Sakuradamon at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Tokyo's Imperial Palace]] is such a gate. * {{nihongo|''Munamon''|棟門}} – A gate formed by two pillars sustaining a gabled roof. Similar to a ''kōraimon'', but lacking the roofed secondary pillars. *{{nihongo|''Nagayamon''|長屋門}} lit. ''nagaya'' gate – A ''[[:en:Nagaya (architecture)|nagaya]]'', literally a long house, was a row house where low status samurai used to live, and the ''nagayamon'' was a gate that allowed traffic from one side of the structure to the other.<ref>{{cite web|title=Nagaya|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagaya.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *''[[:en:Nijūmon|Nijūmon]]'' – A two-storied gate with a pent roof between the two stories. Distinguishable from the similar ''rōmon'' for having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro">{{cite web|title=Rōmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/roumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> *''[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]'' – A gate enshrining in its two outer [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]] the statues of two warden gods, the [[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]. *''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' – A two-storied, single roofed gate where the second story is inaccessible and offers no usable room. Distinguishable from the similar ''nijūmon'' for not having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro"/> *''[[Sanmon]]'' – The most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sanmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sanmon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> Also used by other schools, particularly the Jōdo. Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''sōmon'' (outer gate) and the ''[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the main hall). *''[[:en:Sōmon|Sōmon]]'' – the gate at the entrance of a temple.<ref name="kojien">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It often precedes the bigger and more important ''[[sanmon]]''. *''[[Torii]]'' – This distinctive symbolic gate is usually associated with Shinto shrines; however, it is common at Buddhist temples too, as most have at least one. * {{nihongo|''Uzumimon''|埋門|extra=lit. "buried gate"}} – Gates opened in a castle wall. Because they were used to connect surfaces at different levels, they looked as if they were buried in the ground.<ref>{{cite web|title=Uzumimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/u/uzumimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yaguramon''|櫓門}} – A gate with a ''[[:en:Yagura (tower)|yagura]]'' on top.<ref name="castle.info">{{cite web |url=http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |title=JCastle.info – Modern history |access-date=1 September 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090114222416/http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |archive-date=14 January 2009 }}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yakuimon''|薬医門}} – A gate having no pillars under the ridge of its gabled gate, and supported by four pillars at its corners.<ref name="kojien"/><ref name="yakuimon">{{cite web|title=Yakuimon|publisher=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/y/yakuimon.htm|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Shikyakumon'' or ''Yotsuashimon''|四脚門|four-legged gate}} – so called because of its four secondary pillars which support two main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has six pillars.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shikyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shikyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] {{Short description|Japanese term for gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:NikkoToshoguOmotemon5230.jpg|thumb|270 px|[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]'s ''omote-mon'' (front gate) structurally is a ''hakkyakumon'' (eight-legged gate)]]
{{nihongo|'''''Mon'''''|門|gate}} is a generic Japanese term for [[:en:Gate|Gate]] often used, either alone or as a suffix, in referring to the many gates used by [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and traditional-style buildings and castles.
==Significance== Unlike gates of secular buildings, most temple and shrine gates are purely symbolic elements of [[:en:Liminality|Liminality]], as they cannot be completely closed and just mark the transition between the mundane and the sacred.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Carson |first= Timothy L. |chapter= Seven: Betwixt and Between, Worship and Liminal Reality |title= Transforming Worship |place= St. Louis, MO |publisher= Chalice |year= 2003 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Turner |first= Victor |chapter= Betwixt and Between: The Liminal Period in Rites de Passage |title= The Forest of Symbols |place= Ithaca, NY |publisher= Cornell University Press |year= 1967}}</ref> In many cases, for example that of the ''[[sanmon]]'', a temple gate has purifying, cleansing properties.
==Description== Gate size is measured in ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'', where a ''ken'' is the interval between two pillars of a traditional-style building. A temple's ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' for example can have dimensions from a maximum of 5x2 ''ken'' to a more common 3x2 ''ken'', down to even one ''ken''.<ref>{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | language=ja |page=79}}</ref> The word is usually translated in English as "bay" and is better understood as an indication of proportions than as a unit of measurement.
Like the temples they belong to, gates can be in the ''[[:en:Wayō|Wayō]]'', ''[[daibutsuyō]]'', ''[[:en:Zenshūyō|Zenshūyō]]'' or ''[[setchūyō]]'' style.<ref name="jaanusmon">{{cite web|title=Mon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=27 August 2010}}</ref> They can be named after: * Their location, as the {{nihongo|''chūmon''|中門|lit. "intermediate gate"}} or of the {{nihongo|omotemon|表門|lit. front gate}} or the {{nihongo|karametemon|搦手門|lit. back entrance gate}}. * The deity they house, as the ''[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]'' (lit. "Niō gate", see below), a gate enshrining two gods called [[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]] in its outer bays. * Their structure or shape, as the ''[[:en:Nijūmon|Nijūmon]]'' (lit. "two-story gate", see below) and the ''rōmon'' (lit. tower gate). * Their function, as the ''[[sanmon]]'' (see below), which is the most important gate of a [[:en:Zen|Zen]] or [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] temple.
Not all such terms are mutually exclusive and the same gate may be called with different names according to the situation. For example, a ''Niōmon'' can also be correctly called a ''nijūmon'' if it has two stories.
==Variations== Very different structurally from the others is the ''[[torii]]mon'' (normally called simply ''torii''), a two-legged gate in stone or wood regularly associated with Shinto, but common also within Japanese Buddhist temples.<ref name="Shinto"/> As prominent a temple as [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]'s [[Shitennō-ji]], founded in 593 by [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] and the oldest state-built Buddhist temple in the country, has a ''torii'' straddling one of its entrances.<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=12 February 2010}}</ref> The origins of the ''torii'' are unknown; although several theories on the subject exist, none has gained universal acceptance.<ref name=Shinto>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=280|title=Torii|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]]| date=2005-06-02| access-date=2010-02-21}}</ref> Because the use of symbolic gates is widespread in Asia—such structures can be found for example in [[:en:India|India]], [[:en:China|China]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]], [[:en:Thailand‏‎|Thailand‏‎]], [[:en:Korea|Korea]], and within [[Nicobarese people|Nicobarese]] and [[:en:Shompen people|Shompen]] villages—historians believe it may be an imported tradition. It most often symbolically marks the entrance of a Shinto shrine. For this reason, it is never closed.
==Common types== * {{nihongo|''Hakkyakumon'' or ''Yatsuashimon''|八脚門|eight-legged gate}} – so called because of its eight secondary pillars, which support four main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has twelve pillars altogether.<ref>{{cite web|title=Hakkyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hakkyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Heijūmon''|塀重門}} – A gate in a wall consisting in just two square posts.<ref name="castle.info"/> *{{nihongo|''Kabukimon''|冠木門}} – A gate in a wall formed by two square posts and a horizontal beam.<ref>{{cite web|title=Kabukimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kabukimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''[[Karamon]]''|唐門|chinese gate}} – A gate characterized by a ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan.<ref name="jaanuskaramon">{{cite web|title=Karamon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> ''Karamon'' are used at [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s. *{{nihongo|''Kōraimon''|高麗門|lit. Korean gate}} – Used at castles, temples and ''daimyō'' residences, it consists of a tiled, gabled roof on two pillars, plus two smaller roofs over the {{nihongo|secondary pillars|控柱|hikaebashira}} on the rear of the gate.<ref name="jaanuskaramon"/> * {{nihongo|''Masugata''|枡形}}. A defensive structure consisting in a courtyard along the wall of a castle with two gates set at a square angle, one giving access to the castle and one facing the outside. The external gate is typically a ''kōraimon'', the internal one a ''yaguramon''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Masugata|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/masugata.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> The Sakuradamon at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Tokyo's Imperial Palace]] is such a gate. * {{nihongo|''Munamon''|棟門}} – A gate formed by two pillars sustaining a gabled roof. Similar to a ''kōraimon'', but lacking the roofed secondary pillars. *{{nihongo|''Nagayamon''|長屋門}} lit. ''nagaya'' gate – A ''[[:en:Nagaya (architecture)|nagaya]]'', literally a long house, was a row house where low status samurai used to live, and the ''nagayamon'' was a gate that allowed traffic from one side of the structure to the other.<ref>{{cite web|title=Nagaya|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagaya.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *''[[:en:Nijūmon|Nijūmon]]'' – A two-storied gate with a pent roof between the two stories. Distinguishable from the similar ''rōmon'' for having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro">{{cite web|title=Rōmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/roumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> *''[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]'' – A gate enshrining in its two outer [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]] the statues of two warden gods, the [[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]. *''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' – A two-storied, single roofed gate where the second story is inaccessible and offers no usable room. Distinguishable from the similar ''nijūmon'' for not having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro"/> *''[[Sanmon]]'' – The most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sanmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sanmon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> Also used by other schools, particularly the Jōdo. Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''sōmon'' (outer gate) and the ''[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the main hall). *''[[:en:Sōmon|Sōmon]]'' – the gate at the entrance of a temple.<ref name="kojien">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It often precedes the bigger and more important ''[[sanmon]]''. *''[[Torii]]'' – This distinctive symbolic gate is usually associated with Shinto shrines; however, it is common at Buddhist temples too, as most have at least one. * {{nihongo|''Uzumimon''|埋門|extra=lit. "buried gate"}} – Gates opened in a castle wall. Because they were used to connect surfaces at different levels, they looked as if they were buried in the ground.<ref>{{cite web|title=Uzumimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/u/uzumimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yaguramon''|櫓門}} – A gate with a ''[[:en:Yagura (tower)|yagura]]'' on top.<ref name="castle.info">{{cite web |url=http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |title=JCastle.info – Modern history |access-date=1 September 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090114222416/http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |archive-date=14 January 2009 }}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yakuimon''|薬医門}} – A gate having no pillars under the ridge of its gabled gate, and supported by four pillars at its corners.<ref name="kojien"/><ref name="yakuimon">{{cite web|title=Yakuimon|publisher=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/y/yakuimon.htm|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Shikyakumon'' or ''Yotsuashimon''|四脚門|four-legged gate}} – so called because of its four secondary pillars which support two main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has six pillars.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shikyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shikyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref>
==Photo gallery== <gallery widths=170px heights=130px> File:Horyu-ji02s3200.jpg|''Hakkyakumon'' File:Heijuumon.jpg|''Heijūmon'' File:Kara-mon Gate of Nishi Hongwanji.JPG|''Karamon'' File:Sasaymajo kabukimon.jpg|''Kabukimon'' File:SakuradaGate2.jpg|''Kōraimon'', outside File:Matsumoto Castle09n4592.jpg|''Kōraimon'', inside File:Wakayama Yosuien19n4272.jpg|''Munamon'' File:Nagayamon of Oishi House Ako01n3200.jpg|''Nagayamon'' File:Hagiwaraji 02.JPG|''Niōmon'' File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' is a ''nijūmon'' File:Udo Jingu Roumon.jpg|''Rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Hannyaji Romon01.cropped.jpg|This ''shikyakumon'' is also a ''rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Tatsuno castle03s1960.jpg|An ''uzumimon'' File:Toyooka City Library04st3200.jpg|''Yakuimon'' File:KawanoeJo-Fukugen-YaguraMon.jpg|''Yaguramon'' File:Entrance to Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine.jpg|A red ''torii'' at the entrance of a Shinto shrine </gallery>

"two-story gate", see below) and the ''rōmon'' (lit. tower gate). * Their function, as the ''[[sanmon]]'' (see below), which is the most important gate of a [[:en:Zen|Zen]] or [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] temple.
Not all such terms are mutually exclusive and the same gate may be called with different names according to the situation. For example, a ''Niōmon'' can also be correctly called a ''nijūmon'' if it has two stories.

It most often symbolically marks the entrance of a Shinto shrine. For this reason, it is never closed.
==Common types== * {{nihongo|''Hakkyakumon'' or ''Yatsuashimon''|八脚門|eight-legged gate}} – so called because of its eight secondary pillars, which support four main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has twelve pillars altogether.<ref>{{cite web|title=Hakkyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hakkyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Heijūmon''|塀重門}} – A gate in a wall consisting in just two square posts.<ref name="castle.info"/> *{{nihongo|''Kabukimon''|冠木門}} – A gate in a wall formed by two square posts and a horizontal beam.<ref>{{cite web|title=Kabukimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kabukimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''[[Karamon]]''|唐門|chinese gate}} – A gate characterized by a ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan.<ref name="jaanuskaramon">{{cite web|title=Karamon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> ''Karamon'' are used at [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s. *{{nihongo|''Kōraimon''|高麗門|lit. Korean gate}} – Used at castles, temples and ''daimyō'' residences, it consists of a tiled, gabled roof on two pillars, plus two smaller roofs over the {{nihongo|secondary pillars|控柱|hikaebashira}} on the rear of the gate.<ref name="jaanuskaramon"/> * {{nihongo|''Masugata''|枡形}}. A defensive structure consisting in a courtyard along the wall of a castle with two gates set at a square angle, one giving access to the castle and one facing the outside. The external gate is typically a ''kōraimon'', the internal one a ''yaguramon''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Masugata|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/masugata.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> The Sakuradamon at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Tokyo's Imperial Palace]] is such a gate. * {{nihongo|''Munamon''|棟門}} – A gate formed by two pillars sustaining a gabled roof. Similar to a ''kōraimon'', but lacking the roofed secondary pillars. *{{nihongo|''Nagayamon''|長屋門}} lit. ''nagaya'' gate – A ''[[:en:Nagaya (architecture)|nagaya]]'', literally a long house, was a row house where low status samurai used to live, and the ''nagayamon'' was a gate that allowed traffic from one side of the structure to the other.<ref>{{cite web|title=Nagaya|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagaya.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *''[[:en:Nijūmon|Nijūmon]]'' – A two-storied gate with a pent roof between the two stories. Distinguishable from the similar ''rōmon'' for having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro">{{cite web|title=Rōmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/roumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> *''[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]'' – A gate enshrining in its two outer [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]] the statues of two warden gods, the [[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]. *''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' – A two-storied, single roofed gate where the second story is inaccessible and offers no usable room. Distinguishable from the similar ''nijūmon'' for not having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro"/> *''[[Sanmon]]'' – The most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sanmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sanmon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> Also used by other schools, particularly the Jōdo. Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''sōmon'' (outer gate) and the ''[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the main hall). *''[[:en:Sōmon|Sōmon]]'' – the gate at the entrance of a temple.<ref name="kojien">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It often precedes the bigger and more important ''[[sanmon]]''. *''[[Torii]]'' – This distinctive symbolic gate is usually associated with Shinto shrines; however, it is common at Buddhist temples too, as most have at least one. * {{nihongo|''Uzumimon''|埋門|extra=lit. "buried gate"}} – Gates opened in a castle wall. Because they were used to connect surfaces at different levels, they looked as if they were buried in the ground.<ref>{{cite web|title=Uzumimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/u/uzumimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yaguramon''|櫓門}} – A gate with a ''[[:en:Yagura (tower)|yagura]]'' on top.<ref name="castle.info">{{cite web |url=http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |title=JCastle.info – Modern history |access-date=1 September 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090114222416/http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |archive-date=14 January 2009 }}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yakuimon''|薬医門}} – A gate having no pillars under the ridge of its gabled gate, and supported by four pillars at its corners.<ref name="kojien"/><ref name="yakuimon">{{cite web|title=Yakuimon|publisher=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/y/yakuimon.htm|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Shikyakumon'' or ''Yotsuashimon''|四脚門|four-legged gate}} – so called because of its four secondary pillars which support two main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has six pillars.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shikyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shikyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref>
==Photo gallery== <gallery widths=170px heights=130px> File:Horyu-ji02s3200.jpg|''Hakkyakumon'' File:Heijuumon.jpg|''Heijūmon'' File:Kara-mon Gate of Nishi Hongwanji.JPG|''Karamon'' File:Sasaymajo kabukimon.jpg|''Kabukimon'' File:SakuradaGate2.jpg|''Kōraimon'', outside File:Matsumoto Castle09n4592.jpg|''Kōraimon'', inside File:Wakayama Yosuien19n4272.jpg|''Munamon'' File:Nagayamon of Oishi House Ako01n3200.jpg|''Nagayamon'' File:Hagiwaraji 02.JPG|''Niōmon'' File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' is a ''nijūmon'' File:Udo Jingu Roumon.jpg|''Rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Hannyaji Romon01.cropped.jpg|This ''shikyakumon'' is also a ''rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Tatsuno castle03s1960.jpg|An ''uzumimon'' File:Toyooka City Library04st3200.jpg|''Yakuimon'' File:KawanoeJo-Fukugen-YaguraMon.jpg|''Yaguramon'' File:Entrance to Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine.jpg|A red ''torii'' at the entrance of a Shinto shrine </gallery>
==References== <references/>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] {{Short description|Japanese term for gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:NikkoToshoguOmotemon5230.jpg|thumb|270 px|[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]'s ''omote-mon'' (front gate) structurally is a ''hakkyakumon'' (eight-legged gate)]]
{{nihongo|'''''Mon'''''|門|gate}} is a generic Japanese term for [[:en:Gate|Gate]] often used, either alone or as a suffix, in referring to the many gates used by [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and traditional-style buildings and castles.
==Significance== Unlike gates of secular buildings, most temple and shrine gates are purely symbolic elements of [[:en:Liminality|Liminality]], as they cannot be completely closed and just mark the transition between the mundane and the sacred.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Carson |first= Timothy L. |chapter= Seven: Betwixt and Between, Worship and Liminal Reality |title= Transforming Worship |place= St. Louis, MO |publisher= Chalice |year= 2003 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Turner |first= Victor |chapter= Betwixt and Between: The Liminal Period in Rites de Passage |title= The Forest of Symbols |place= Ithaca, NY |publisher= Cornell University Press |year= 1967}}</ref> In many cases, for example that of the ''[[sanmon]]'', a temple gate has purifying, cleansing properties.
==Description== Gate size is measured in ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'', where a ''ken'' is the interval between two pillars of a traditional-style building. A temple's ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' for example can have dimensions from a maximum of 5x2 ''ken'' to a more common 3x2 ''ken'', down to even one ''ken''.<ref>{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | language=ja |page=79}}</ref> The word is usually translated in English as "bay" and is better understood as an indication of proportions than as a unit of measurement.
Like the temples they belong to, gates can be in the ''[[:en:Wayō|Wayō]]'', ''[[daibutsuyō]]'', ''[[:en:Zenshūyō|Zenshūyō]]'' or ''[[setchūyō]]'' style.<ref name="jaanusmon">{{cite web|title=Mon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=27 August 2010}}</ref> They can be named after: * Their location, as the {{nihongo|''chūmon''|中門|lit. "intermediate gate"}} or of the {{nihongo|omotemon|表門|lit. front gate}} or the {{nihongo|karametemon|搦手門|lit. back entrance gate}}. * The deity they house, as the ''[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]'' (lit. "Niō gate", see below), a gate enshrining two gods called [[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]] in its outer bays. * Their structure or shape, as the ''[[:en:Nijūmon|Nijūmon]]'' (lit. "two-story gate", see below) and the ''rōmon'' (lit. tower gate). * Their function, as the ''[[sanmon]]'' (see below), which is the most important gate of a [[:en:Zen|Zen]] or [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] temple.
Not all such terms are mutually exclusive and the same gate may be called with different names according to the situation. For example, a ''Niōmon'' can also be correctly called a ''nijūmon'' if it has two stories.
==Variations== Very different structurally from the others is the ''[[torii]]mon'' (normally called simply ''torii''), a two-legged gate in stone or wood regularly associated with Shinto, but common also within Japanese Buddhist temples.<ref name="Shinto"/> As prominent a temple as [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]'s [[Shitennō-ji]], founded in 593 by [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] and the oldest state-built Buddhist temple in the country, has a ''torii'' straddling one of its entrances.<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=12 February 2010}}</ref> The origins of the ''torii'' are unknown; although several theories on the subject exist, none has gained universal acceptance.<ref name=Shinto>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=280|title=Torii|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]]| date=2005-06-02| access-date=2010-02-21}}</ref> Because the use of symbolic gates is widespread in Asia—such structures can be found for example in [[:en:India|India]], [[:en:China|China]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]], [[:en:Thailand‏‎|Thailand‏‎]], [[:en:Korea|Korea]], and within [[Nicobarese people|Nicobarese]] and [[:en:Shompen people|Shompen]] villages—historians believe it may be an imported tradition. It most often symbolically marks the entrance of a Shinto shrine. For this reason, it is never closed.
==Common types== * {{nihongo|''Hakkyakumon'' or ''Yatsuashimon''|八脚門|eight-legged gate}} – so called because of its eight secondary pillars, which support four main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has twelve pillars altogether.<ref>{{cite web|title=Hakkyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hakkyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Heijūmon''|塀重門}} – A gate in a wall consisting in just two square posts.<ref name="castle.info"/> *{{nihongo|''Kabukimon''|冠木門}} – A gate in a wall formed by two square posts and a horizontal beam.<ref>{{cite web|title=Kabukimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kabukimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''[[Karamon]]''|唐門|chinese gate}} – A gate characterized by a ''[[:en:Karahafu|Karahafu]]'', an undulating [[:en:Bargeboard|Bargeboard]] peculiar to Japan.<ref name="jaanuskaramon">{{cite web|title=Karamon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> ''Karamon'' are used at [[:en:Japanese castle|Japanese castle]]s, [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s. *{{nihongo|''Kōraimon''|高麗門|lit. Korean gate}} – Used at castles, temples and ''daimyō'' residences, it consists of a tiled, gabled roof on two pillars, plus two smaller roofs over the {{nihongo|secondary pillars|控柱|hikaebashira}} on the rear of the gate.<ref name="jaanuskaramon"/> * {{nihongo|''Masugata''|枡形}}. A defensive structure consisting in a courtyard along the wall of a castle with two gates set at a square angle, one giving access to the castle and one facing the outside. The external gate is typically a ''kōraimon'', the internal one a ''yaguramon''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Masugata|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/masugata.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> The Sakuradamon at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Tokyo's Imperial Palace]] is such a gate. * {{nihongo|''Munamon''|棟門}} – A gate formed by two pillars sustaining a gabled roof. Similar to a ''kōraimon'', but lacking the roofed secondary pillars. *{{nihongo|''Nagayamon''|長屋門}} lit. ''nagaya'' gate – A ''[[:en:Nagaya (architecture)|nagaya]]'', literally a long house, was a row house where low status samurai used to live, and the ''nagayamon'' was a gate that allowed traffic from one side of the structure to the other.<ref>{{cite web|title=Nagaya|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagaya.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *''[[:en:Nijūmon|Nijūmon]]'' – A two-storied gate with a pent roof between the two stories. Distinguishable from the similar ''rōmon'' for having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro">{{cite web|title=Rōmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/roumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> *''[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]'' – A gate enshrining in its two outer [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]] the statues of two warden gods, the [[:en:Nio (Buddhism)|Nio (Buddhism)]]. *''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' – A two-storied, single roofed gate where the second story is inaccessible and offers no usable room. Distinguishable from the similar ''nijūmon'' for not having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro"/> *''[[Sanmon]]'' – The most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sanmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sanmon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> Also used by other schools, particularly the Jōdo. Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''sōmon'' (outer gate) and the ''[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the main hall). *''[[:en:Sōmon|Sōmon]]'' – the gate at the entrance of a temple.<ref name="kojien">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It often precedes the bigger and more important ''[[sanmon]]''. *''[[Torii]]'' – This distinctive symbolic gate is usually associated with Shinto shrines; however, it is common at Buddhist temples too, as most have at least one. * {{nihongo|''Uzumimon''|埋門|extra=lit. "buried gate"}} – Gates opened in a castle wall. Because they were used to connect surfaces at different levels, they looked as if they were buried in the ground.<ref>{{cite web|title=Uzumimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/u/uzumimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yaguramon''|櫓門}} – A gate with a ''[[:en:Yagura (tower)|yagura]]'' on top.<ref name="castle.info">{{cite web |url=http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |title=JCastle.info – Modern history |access-date=1 September 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090114222416/http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |archive-date=14 January 2009 }}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yakuimon''|薬医門}} – A gate having no pillars under the ridge of its gabled gate, and supported by four pillars at its corners.<ref name="kojien"/><ref name="yakuimon">{{cite web|title=Yakuimon|publisher=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/y/yakuimon.htm|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Shikyakumon'' or ''Yotsuashimon''|四脚門|four-legged gate}} – so called because of its four secondary pillars which support two main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has six pillars.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shikyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shikyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref>
==Photo gallery== <gallery widths=170px heights=130px> File:Horyu-ji02s3200.jpg|''Hakkyakumon'' File:Heijuumon.jpg|''Heijūmon'' File:Kara-mon Gate of Nishi Hongwanji.JPG|''Karamon'' File:Sasaymajo kabukimon.jpg|''Kabukimon'' File:SakuradaGate2.jpg|''Kōraimon'', outside File:Matsumoto Castle09n4592.jpg|''Kōraimon'', inside File:Wakayama Yosuien19n4272.jpg|''Munamon'' File:Nagayamon of Oishi House Ako01n3200.jpg|''Nagayamon'' File:Hagiwaraji 02.JPG|''Niōmon'' File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' is a ''nijūmon'' File:Udo Jingu Roumon.jpg|''Rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Hannyaji Romon01.cropped.jpg|This ''shikyakumon'' is also a ''rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Tatsuno castle03s1960.jpg|An ''uzumimon'' File:Toyooka City Library04st3200.jpg|''Yakuimon'' File:KawanoeJo-Fukugen-YaguraMon.jpg|''Yaguramon'' File:Entrance to Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine.jpg|A red ''torii'' at the entrance of a Shinto shrine </gallery>

{{nihongo|'''''Mon'''''|門|gate}} is a generic Japanese term for {{ill|Gate|en|Gate|WD=Q53060|lt=Gate}} often used, either alone or as a suffix, in referring to the many gates used by {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}}, {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} and traditional-style buildings and castles.
==Significance== Unlike gates of secular buildings, most temple and shrine gates are purely symbolic elements of {{ill|Liminality|en|Liminality|WD=Q1825499|lt=Liminality}}, as they cannot be completely closed and just mark the transition between the mundane and the sacred.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Carson |first= Timothy L. |chapter= Seven: Betwixt and Between, Worship and Liminal Reality |title= Transforming Worship |place= St. Louis, MO |publisher= Chalice |year= 2003 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Turner |first= Victor |chapter= Betwixt and Between: The Liminal Period in Rites de Passage |title= The Forest of Symbols |place= Ithaca, NY |publisher= Cornell University Press |year= 1967}}</ref> In many cases, for example that of the ''[[sanmon]]'', a temple gate has purifying, cleansing properties.
==Description== Gate size is measured in ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'', where a ''ken'' is the interval between two pillars of a traditional-style building. A temple's ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'' for example can have dimensions from a maximum of 5x2 ''ken'' to a more common 3x2 ''ken'', down to even one ''ken''.<ref>{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | language=ja |page=79}}</ref> The word is usually translated in English as "bay" and is better understood as an indication of proportions than as a unit of measurement.
Like the temples they belong to, gates can be in the ''{{ill|Wayō|en|Wayō|WD=Q3566879|lt=Wayō}}'', ''[[daibutsuyō]]'', ''{{ill|Zenshūyō|en|Zenshūyō|WD=Q3575224|lt=Zenshūyō}}'' or ''[[setchūyō]]'' style.<ref name="jaanusmon">{{cite web|title=Mon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=27 August 2010}}</ref> They can be named after: * Their location, as the {{nihongo|''chūmon''|中門|lit. "intermediate gate"}} or of the {{nihongo|omotemon|表門|lit. front gate}} or the {{nihongo|karametemon|搦手門|lit. back entrance gate}}. * The deity they house, as the ''{{ill|Niōmon|en|Niōmon|WD=Q3342330|lt=Niōmon}}'' (lit. "Niō gate", see below), a gate enshrining two gods called {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)}} in its outer bays. * Their structure or shape, as the ''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|WD=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon}}'' (lit. "two-story gate", see below) and the ''rōmon'' (lit. tower gate). * Their function, as the ''[[sanmon]]'' (see below), which is the most important gate of a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} or [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] temple.
Not all such terms are mutually exclusive and the same gate may be called with different names according to the situation. For example, a ''Niōmon'' can also be correctly called a ''nijūmon'' if it has two stories.
==Variations== Very different structurally from the others is the ''[[torii]]mon'' (normally called simply ''torii''), a two-legged gate in stone or wood regularly associated with Shinto, but common also within Japanese Buddhist temples.<ref name="Shinto"/> As prominent a temple as {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}'s [[Shitennō-ji]], founded in 593 by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} and the oldest state-built Buddhist temple in the country, has a ''torii'' straddling one of its entrances.<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=12 February 2010}}</ref> The origins of the ''torii'' are unknown; although several theories on the subject exist, none has gained universal acceptance.<ref name=Shinto>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=280|title=Torii|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]]| date=2005-06-02| access-date=2010-02-21}}</ref> Because the use of symbolic gates is widespread in Asia—such structures can be found for example in {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India}}, {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}, {{ill|Vietnam|en|Vietnam|WD=Q881|lt=Vietnam}}, {{ill|Thailand‏‎|en|Thailand‏‎|WD=Q869|lt=Thailand‏‎}}, {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}}, and within [[Nicobarese people|Nicobarese]] and {{ill|Shompen people|en|Shompen people|WD=Q274386|lt=Shompen}} villages—historians believe it may be an imported tradition. It most often symbolically marks the entrance of a Shinto shrine. For this reason, it is never closed.
==Common types== * {{nihongo|''Hakkyakumon'' or ''Yatsuashimon''|八脚門|eight-legged gate}} – so called because of its eight secondary pillars, which support four main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has twelve pillars altogether.<ref>{{cite web|title=Hakkyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hakkyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Heijūmon''|塀重門}} – A gate in a wall consisting in just two square posts.<ref name="castle.info"/> *{{nihongo|''Kabukimon''|冠木門}} – A gate in a wall formed by two square posts and a horizontal beam.<ref>{{cite web|title=Kabukimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kabukimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''[[Karamon]]''|唐門|chinese gate}} – A gate characterized by a ''{{ill|Karahafu|en|Karahafu|WD=Q977293|lt=Karahafu}}'', an undulating {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard}} peculiar to Japan.<ref name="jaanuskaramon">{{cite web|title=Karamon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> ''Karamon'' are used at {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}}s, {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} and {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s. *{{nihongo|''Kōraimon''|高麗門|lit. Korean gate}} – Used at castles, temples and ''daimyō'' residences, it consists of a tiled, gabled roof on two pillars, plus two smaller roofs over the {{nihongo|secondary pillars|控柱|hikaebashira}} on the rear of the gate.<ref name="jaanuskaramon"/> * {{nihongo|''Masugata''|枡形}}. A defensive structure consisting in a courtyard along the wall of a castle with two gates set at a square angle, one giving access to the castle and one facing the outside. The external gate is typically a ''kōraimon'', the internal one a ''yaguramon''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Masugata|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/masugata.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> The Sakuradamon at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Tokyo's Imperial Palace]] is such a gate. * {{nihongo|''Munamon''|棟門}} – A gate formed by two pillars sustaining a gabled roof. Similar to a ''kōraimon'', but lacking the roofed secondary pillars. *{{nihongo|''Nagayamon''|長屋門}} lit. ''nagaya'' gate – A ''{{ill|Nagaya (architecture)|en|Nagaya (architecture)|WD=Q93154241|lt=nagaya}}'', literally a long house, was a row house where low status samurai used to live, and the ''nagayamon'' was a gate that allowed traffic from one side of the structure to the other.<ref>{{cite web|title=Nagaya|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagaya.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|WD=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon}}'' – A two-storied gate with a pent roof between the two stories. Distinguishable from the similar ''rōmon'' for having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro">{{cite web|title=Rōmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/roumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> *''{{ill|Niōmon|en|Niōmon|WD=Q3342330|lt=Niōmon}}'' – A gate enshrining in its two outer {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bays}} the statues of two warden gods, the {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)}}. *''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'' – A two-storied, single roofed gate where the second story is inaccessible and offers no usable room. Distinguishable from the similar ''nijūmon'' for not having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro"/> *''[[Sanmon]]'' – The most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sanmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sanmon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> Also used by other schools, particularly the Jōdo. Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''sōmon'' (outer gate) and the ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the main hall). *''{{ill|Sōmon|en|Sōmon|WD=Q3511697|lt=Sōmon}}'' – the gate at the entrance of a temple.<ref name="kojien">Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It often precedes the bigger and more important ''[[sanmon]]''. *''[[Torii]]'' – This distinctive symbolic gate is usually associated with Shinto shrines; however, it is common at Buddhist temples too, as most have at least one. * {{nihongo|''Uzumimon''|埋門|extra=lit. "buried gate"}} – Gates opened in a castle wall. Because they were used to connect surfaces at different levels, they looked as if they were buried in the ground.<ref>{{cite web|title=Uzumimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/u/uzumimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yaguramon''|櫓門}} – A gate with a ''{{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=yagura}}'' on top.<ref name="castle.info">{{cite web |url=http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |title=JCastle.info – Modern history |access-date=1 September 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090114222416/http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |archive-date=14 January 2009 }}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yakuimon''|薬医門}} – A gate having no pillars under the ridge of its gabled gate, and supported by four pillars at its corners.<ref name="kojien"/><ref name="yakuimon">{{cite web|title=Yakuimon|publisher=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/y/yakuimon.htm|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Shikyakumon'' or ''Yotsuashimon''|四脚門|four-legged gate}} – so called because of its four secondary pillars which support two main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has six pillars.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shikyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shikyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref>
==Photo gallery== <gallery widths=170px heights=130px> File:Horyu-ji02s3200.jpg|''Hakkyakumon'' File:Heijuumon.jpg|''Heijūmon'' File:Kara-mon Gate of Nishi Hongwanji.JPG|''Karamon'' File:Sasaymajo kabukimon.jpg|''Kabukimon'' File:SakuradaGate2.jpg|''Kōraimon'', outside File:Matsumoto Castle09n4592.jpg|''Kōraimon'', inside File:Wakayama Yosuien19n4272.jpg|''Munamon'' File:Nagayamon of Oishi House Ako01n3200.jpg|''Nagayamon'' File:Hagiwaraji 02.JPG|''Niōmon'' File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' is a ''nijūmon'' File:Udo Jingu Roumon.jpg|''Rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Hannyaji Romon01.cropped.jpg|This ''shikyakumon'' is also a ''rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Tatsuno castle03s1960.jpg|An ''uzumimon'' File:Toyooka City Library04st3200.jpg|''Yakuimon'' File:KawanoeJo-Fukugen-YaguraMon.jpg|''Yaguramon'' File:Entrance to Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine.jpg|A red ''torii'' at the entrance of a Shinto shrine </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Μον (αρχιτεκτονική)]][[en:Mon (architecture)]][[fr:Mon (architecture)]][[mr:मॉन (वास्तुकला)]][[ms:Mon (seni bina)]][[ru:Мон (ворота)]][[sl:Mon (arhitektura)]] {{Short description|Japanese term for gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:NikkoToshoguOmotemon5230.jpg|thumb|270 px|{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}'s ''omote-mon'' (front gate) structurally is a ''hakkyakumon'' (eight-legged gate)]]
{{nihongo|'''''Mon'''''|門|gate}} is a generic Japanese term for {{ill|Gate|en|Gate|WD=Q53060|lt=Gate}} often used, either alone or as a suffix, in referring to the many gates used by {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}}, {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} and traditional-style buildings and castles.
==Significance== Unlike gates of secular buildings, most temple and shrine gates are purely symbolic elements of {{ill|Liminality|en|Liminality|WD=Q1825499|lt=Liminality}}, as they cannot be completely closed and just mark the transition between the mundane and the sacred.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Carson |first= Timothy L. |chapter= Seven: Betwixt and Between, Worship and Liminal Reality |title= Transforming Worship |place= St. Louis, MO |publisher= Chalice |year= 2003 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Turner |first= Victor |chapter= Betwixt and Between: The Liminal Period in Rites de Passage |title= The Forest of Symbols |place= Ithaca, NY |publisher= Cornell University Press |year= 1967}}</ref> In many cases, for example that of the ''[[sanmon]]'', a temple gate has purifying, cleansing properties.
==Description== Gate size is measured in ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'', where a ''ken'' is the interval between two pillars of a traditional-style building. A temple's ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'' for example can have dimensions from a maximum of 5x2 ''ken'' to a more common 3x2 ''ken'', down to even one ''ken''.<ref>{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | language=ja |page=79}}</ref> The word is usually translated in English as "bay" and is better understood as an indication of proportions than as a unit of measurement.
Like the temples they belong to, gates can be in the ''{{ill|Wayō|en|Wayō|WD=Q3566879|lt=Wayō}}'', ''[[daibutsuyō]]'', ''{{ill|Zenshūyō|en|Zenshūyō|WD=Q3575224|lt=Zenshūyō}}'' or ''[[setchūyō]]'' style.<ref name="jaanusmon">{{cite web|title=Mon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=27 August 2010}}</ref> They can be named after: * Their location, as the {{nihongo|''chūmon''|中門|lit. "intermediate gate"}} or of the {{nihongo|omotemon|表門|lit. front gate}} or the {{nihongo|karametemon|搦手門|lit. back entrance gate}}. * The deity they house, as the ''{{ill|Niōmon|en|Niōmon|WD=Q3342330|lt=Niōmon}}'' (lit. "Niō gate", see below), a gate enshrining two gods called {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)}} in its outer bays. * Their structure or shape, as the ''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|WD=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon}}'' (lit. "two-story gate", see below) and the ''rōmon'' (lit. tower gate). * Their function, as the ''[[sanmon]]'' (see below), which is the most important gate of a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} or [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] temple.
Not all such terms are mutually exclusive and the same gate may be called with different names according to the situation. For example, a ''Niōmon'' can also be correctly called a ''nijūmon'' if it has two stories.
==Variations== Very different structurally from the others is the ''[[torii]]mon'' (normally called simply ''torii''), a two-legged gate in stone or wood regularly associated with Shinto, but common also within Japanese Buddhist temples.<ref name="Shinto"/> As prominent a temple as {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}'s [[Shitennō-ji]], founded in 593 by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} and the oldest state-built Buddhist temple in the country, has a ''torii'' straddling one of its entrances.<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=12 February 2010}}</ref> The origins of the ''torii'' are unknown; although several theories on the subject exist, none has gained universal acceptance.<ref name=Shinto>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=280|title=Torii|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]]| date=2005-06-02| access-date=2010-02-21}}</ref> Because the use of symbolic gates is widespread in Asia—such structures can be found for example in {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India}}, {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}, {{ill|Vietnam|en|Vietnam|WD=Q881|lt=Vietnam}}, {{ill|Thailand‏‎|en|Thailand‏‎|WD=Q869|lt=Thailand‏‎}}, {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}}, and within [[Nicobarese people|Nicobarese]] and {{ill|Shompen people|en|Shompen people|WD=Q274386|lt=Shompen}} villages—historians believe it may be an imported tradition. It most often symbolically marks the entrance of a Shinto shrine. For this reason, it is never closed.
==Common types== * {{nihongo|''Hakkyakumon'' or ''Yatsuashimon''|八脚門|eight-legged gate}} – so called because of its eight secondary pillars, which support four main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has twelve pillars altogether.<ref>{{cite web|title=Hakkyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hakkyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Heijūmon''|塀重門}} – A gate in a wall consisting in just two square posts.<ref name="castle.info"/> *{{nihongo|''Kabukimon''|冠木門}} – A gate in a wall formed by two square posts and a horizontal beam.<ref>{{cite web|title=Kabukimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kabukimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''[[Karamon]]''|唐門|chinese gate}} – A gate characterized by a ''{{ill|Karahafu|en|Karahafu|WD=Q977293|lt=Karahafu}}'', an undulating {{ill|Bargeboard|en|Bargeboard|WD=Q4881511|lt=Bargeboard}} peculiar to Japan.<ref name="jaanuskaramon">{{cite web|title=Karamon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karamon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> ''Karamon'' are used at {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|WD=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle}}s, {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} and {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s. *{{nihongo|''Kōraimon''|高麗門|lit. Korean gate}} – Used at castles, temples and ''daimyō'' residences, it consists of a tiled, gabled roof on two pillars, plus two smaller roofs over the {{nihongo|secondary pillars|控柱|hikaebashira}} on the rear of the gate.<ref name="jaanuskaramon"/> * {{nihongo|''Masugata''|枡形}}. A defensive structure consisting in a courtyard along the wall of a castle with two gates set at a square angle, one giving access to the castle and one facing the outside. The external gate is typically a ''kōraimon'', the internal one a ''yaguramon''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Masugata|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/masugata.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> The Sakuradamon at [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Tokyo's Imperial Palace]] is such a gate. * {{nihongo|''Munamon''|棟門}} – A gate formed by two pillars sustaining a gabled roof. Similar to a ''kōraimon'', but lacking the roofed secondary pillars. *{{nihongo|''Nagayamon''|長屋門}} lit. ''nagaya'' gate – A ''{{ill|Nagaya (architecture)|en|Nagaya (architecture)|WD=Q93154241|lt=nagaya}}'', literally a long house, was a row house where low status samurai used to live, and the ''nagayamon'' was a gate that allowed traffic from one side of the structure to the other.<ref>{{cite web|title=Nagaya|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/n/nagaya.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|WD=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon}}'' – A two-storied gate with a pent roof between the two stories. Distinguishable from the similar ''rōmon'' for having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro">{{cite web|title=Rōmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/roumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> *''{{ill|Niōmon|en|Niōmon|WD=Q3342330|lt=Niōmon}}'' – A gate enshrining in its two outer {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bays}} the statues of two warden gods, the {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)}}. *''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'' – A two-storied, single roofed gate where the second story is inaccessible and offers no usable room. Distinguishable from the similar ''nijūmon'' for not having a pent roof between stories.<ref name="jaro"/> *''[[Sanmon]]'' – The most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sanmon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/sanmon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=3 September 2010}}</ref> Also used by other schools, particularly the Jōdo. Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''sōmon'' (outer gate) and the ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the main hall). *''{{ill|Sōmon|en|Sōmon|WD=Q3511697|lt=Sōmon}}'' – the gate at the entrance of a temple.<ref name="kojien">Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> It often precedes the bigger and more important ''[[sanmon]]''. *''[[Torii]]'' – This distinctive symbolic gate is usually associated with Shinto shrines; however, it is common at Buddhist temples too, as most have at least one. * {{nihongo|''Uzumimon''|埋門|extra=lit. "buried gate"}} – Gates opened in a castle wall. Because they were used to connect surfaces at different levels, they looked as if they were buried in the ground.<ref>{{cite web|title=Uzumimon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/u/uzumimon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yaguramon''|櫓門}} – A gate with a ''{{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|WD=Q603192|lt=yagura}}'' on top.<ref name="castle.info">{{cite web |url=http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |title=JCastle.info – Modern history |access-date=1 September 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090114222416/http://www.jcastle.info/resources/view/19-Gates |archive-date=14 January 2009 }}</ref> *{{nihongo|''Yakuimon''|薬医門}} – A gate having no pillars under the ridge of its gabled gate, and supported by four pillars at its corners.<ref name="kojien"/><ref name="yakuimon">{{cite web|title=Yakuimon|publisher=JAANUS|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/y/yakuimon.htm|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref> * {{nihongo|''Shikyakumon'' or ''Yotsuashimon''|四脚門|four-legged gate}} – so called because of its four secondary pillars which support two main pillars standing under the gate's ridge. It therefore really has six pillars.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shikyakumon|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shikyakumon.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=1 September 2010}}</ref>
==Photo gallery== <gallery widths=170px heights=130px> File:Horyu-ji02s3200.jpg|''Hakkyakumon'' File:Heijuumon.jpg|''Heijūmon'' File:Kara-mon Gate of Nishi Hongwanji.JPG|''Karamon'' File:Sasaymajo kabukimon.jpg|''Kabukimon'' File:SakuradaGate2.jpg|''Kōraimon'', outside File:Matsumoto Castle09n4592.jpg|''Kōraimon'', inside File:Wakayama Yosuien19n4272.jpg|''Munamon'' File:Nagayamon of Oishi House Ako01n3200.jpg|''Nagayamon'' File:Hagiwaraji 02.JPG|''Niōmon'' File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' is a ''nijūmon'' File:Udo Jingu Roumon.jpg|''Rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Hannyaji Romon01.cropped.jpg|This ''shikyakumon'' is also a ''rōmon''. Note the absence of stairs to the second story. File:Tatsuno castle03s1960.jpg|An ''uzumimon'' File:Toyooka City Library04st3200.jpg|''Yakuimon'' File:KawanoeJo-Fukugen-YaguraMon.jpg|''Yaguramon'' File:Entrance to Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine.jpg|A red ''torii'' at the entrance of a Shinto shrine </gallery>
